text: The patient is positioned in a semi-decubitus orientation to facilitate the hanging technique for laparoscopic splenectomy.
The patient is positioned in a semi-decubitus orientation to facilitate the hanging technique for laparoscopic splenectomy.
The patient is positioned in a semi-decubitus position for the hanging technique of splenectomy, with the surgical table tilted to elevate the left side, optimizing access to the left upper quadrant.
The patient is positioned in a semi-decubitus position, which is enhanced by tilting the surgical table to elevate the left side, facilitating the hanging technique for the splenectomy.
The patient is positioned in an almost left lateral decubitus position, causing the spleen to fall medially within the surgical field.
The patient is positioned in an almost left lateral decubitus position, causing the spleen to fall medially within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a retractor or instrument to lift and hang the spleen, providing better access and visibility for the splenectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a retractor or instrument to lift and hang the spleen, providing better access for the splenectomy procedure.
The spleen is suspended using a retractor, facilitating the dissection of the splenic vessels by providing clear access to the vascular structures.
The spleen is suspended using a retractor, facilitating the dissection of the splenic vessels in the semi-decubitus position.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac by dividing the short gastric vessels, starting from the midpoint of the greater curvature of the stomach and extending to the uppermost short gastric vessels between the upper pole of the spleen and the gastric fundus.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac by dividing the short gastric vessels, starting from the midpoint of the greater curvature of the stomach and extending to the uppermost short gastric vessels between the upper pole of the spleen and the gastric fundus.

The surgeon carefully dissects the uppermost short gastric vessels near the upper pole of the spleen, taking extra caution due to limited space and the risk of vessel avulsion, especially in cases with large splenic lesions or enlarged spleens.
The surgeon carefully dissects the uppermost short gastric vessels near the large splenic lesion, navigating the limited space to prevent avulsion and potential bleeding from the vessels.
The surgeon divides the uppermost short gastric vessels and dissects the upper pole of the spleen away from the gastric fundus, revealing the separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon divides the uppermost short gastric vessels and dissects the upper pole of the spleen away from the gastric fundus, with the spleen positioned medially.
The surgeon decompresses a large splenic cyst to create space in the left upper quadrant, facilitating the procedure. The splenic artery is ligated first, leading to visible shrinkage of the spleen.
The surgeon addresses a large spleen cyst, with the spleen lifted using a retractor or instrument to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon decompresses the splenic cyst to create space in the left upper quadrant, preparing for further surgical steps. The splenic artery is ligated to reduce the spleen's size, facilitating the procedure.

The surgeon places a hitching stitch on the posterior wall of the stomach, securing it to the anterior abdominal wall to keep the stomach away from the operative field, facilitating comfortable dissection of the splenic vessels in the lesser sac.
The surgeon retracts the stomach away from the operative field to provide clear access for dissecting the splenic vessels within the bed of the lesser sac.
The surgeon places a hitching stitch on the posterior wall of the stomach, securing it to the anterior abdominal wall to keep the stomach away from the operative field.
The surgeon places a hitching stitch on the posterior wall of the stomach, closer to the lesser curve than the greater curve, to improve exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon places a hitching stitch closer to the lesser curve of the stomach than the greater curve to improve exposure of the surgical field.
After dividing the short gastric vessels and lifting the stomach, the surgeon identifies the splenic vessels at the upper border of the body and tail of the pancreas.
After dividing the short gastric vessels and lifting the stomach, the surgeon focuses on dissecting the splenic vessels, which are visible in the operative field.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vessels at the upper border of the body and tail of the pancreas.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum at the upper border of the pancreas and begins the dissection of the splenic artery, which is visible at this stage of the splenectomy procedure.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum at the upper border of the pancreas and begins the dissection of the splenic artery.
The surgeon ligates the splenic artery before the vein, which results in the visible shrinkage of the spleen, facilitating easier handling during the procedure.
The surgeon ligates the splenic artery before the vein, causing the spleen to shrink in size, which facilitates easier handling during the procedure.

The surgeon examines the branching pattern of the splenic artery, noting the presence of either a single branch or multiple polar branches at the dissection site near the upper border of the pancreas.
The surgeon examines the branching pattern of the splenic artery, observing either a single branch or multiple polar branches at the dissection site near the upper border of the pancreas.
The surgeon securely clips the splenic artery using hemlocks and divides it, with the option to clip the artery in continuity without division.
The surgeon securely clips the splenic artery using hemlocks and then divides it, following the dissection initiated at the upper border of the pancreas.
The surgeon repositions the artery at the hilum on the splenic surface, preparing for further dissection or ligation.
The surgeon repositions to retake the splenic artery at the hilum, near the splenic surface.
After dividing the splenic artery, the surgeon begins dissecting the splenic vein, preparing for its ligation.
After dividing the splenic artery, the surgeon begins dissecting the splenic vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects the splenic vein, which appears dilated and thin-walled, a critical step in laparoscopic splenectomy, particularly in patients with enlarged spleens.
The surgeon carefully dissects the splenic vein, which appears dilated and thin-walled, a critical step in laparoscopic splenectomy, especially in patients with enlarged spleens.
The surgeon identifies potential collateral vessels around the splenic vein and branches entering the pancreas, which pose a risk of tearing and difficult bleeding during dissection.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the splenic vein, identifying potential collateral vessels and branches entering the pancreas that could be torn, leading to bleeding complications.
The surgeon ligates the splenic artery and proceeds to take the splenic vessels at the splenic hilum where they enter, bypassing the dissection of the main splenic vein.
The surgeon focuses on the splenic hilum, where the splenic vessels enter, preparing for further dissection or ligation.
The surgeon clips, divides, and ligates multiple branches containing both the artery and vein at the splenic hilum, avoiding dissection at the pancreatic bed.
The surgeon clips, divides, and ligates multiple branches containing both the splenic artery and vein, avoiding dissection at the pancreatic bed.

The surgeon employs a hanging maneuver with the patient in lateral decubitus position, lifting the spleen to facilitate the dissection of the splenic vein. If the vein is safely dissected, the surgeon prefers ligating it with a tie rather than using clips or a hemlock.
The surgeon performs a hanging maneuver with the patient in lateral decubitus position, lifting the spleen to facilitate dissection around the splenic vein and artery.
The surgeon safely dissects the splenic vein and prepares to ligate it with a tie, avoiding the use of clips or a hemlock.
The surgeon places a tie around the spinny quain, visibly narrowing it, allowing for the comfortable application of a hemlock or clip.
The surgeon identifies the left gastroepiploic artery, a branch of the splenic artery, arising near the splenic hilum and proceeds to divide it to free the lower end of the spleen.
The surgeon identifies the left gastroepiploic artery, a branch of the splenic artery, arising near the splenic hilum and proceeds to divide it to free the lower end of the spleen.

The surgeon identifies a vein dividing into its tributaries and individually ligates each tributary for better venous control, avoiding the higher risk of clip slippage and bleeding associated with taking the vein at the confluence.
The surgeon identifies a vein dividing into its tributaries and individually ligates each tributary for improved venous control, rather than attempting to ligate the vein at its confluence.

The surgeon proceeds to separate the pancreas from the splenic hilum after successfully dividing the splenic vein, using careful dissection techniques to ensure clear separation of these anatomical structures.
After dividing the splenic vein, the surgeon proceeds to separate the pancreas from the splenic hilum, carefully dissecting the tissue connections between these structures.

The surgeon carefully exposes the pancreatic tail deeply embedded in the splenic hilum, taking care to avoid transecting the pancreatic tissue to prevent potential duct opening and subsequent pancreatic leak or fistula.
The pancreatic tail is visibly embedded deep into the splenic hilum in this patient, requiring careful dissection to separate it from the spleen.
The surgeon carefully dissects the loose irregular tissue to separate the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum, ensuring the pancreatic parenchyma remains intact and the tail is not amputated.
The surgeon carefully dissects the loose irregular tissue to separate the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum, avoiding trauma to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon gently separates the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum, successfully moving it away. The spleen is suspended to maintain tissue traction, aiding in the dissection process.
The surgeon gently separates the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum, successfully moving it away from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon maintains traction on the spleen, facilitating the dissection process by keeping the surrounding tissues taut.

The spleen is suspended by the linoleum ligament and diaphragmatic attachments after the short gastric vessels, splenic vessels, and pancreas have been separated from the splenic hilum. These attachments are typically avascular and can be easily divided, except in cases of portal hypertension.
The spleen is now only attached by the linoleum ligament and its diaphragmatic attachments after the short gastric vessels, splenic vessels, and pancreas have been separated from the splenic hilum.
The surgeon divides the avascular tissues surrounding the spleen, which are typically easy to separate unless portal hypertension is present.
The surgeon separates inflammatory adhesions from the diaphragmatic surface of the diseased and massively enlarged spleen, completing the dissection.
The surgeon separates inflammatory adhesions from the diaphragmatic surface of the massively enlarged spleen, preparing for its removal.

The surgeon checks the hemostasis of the splenic bed and vascular stumps following the completion of the splenectomy, ensuring no bleeding is present as the anesthetist adjusts the patient's blood pressure.
The surgeon inspects the splenic bed and vascular stumps, ensuring hemostasis while the anesthetist adjusts the patient's blood pressure.
The surgeon retrieves the spleen through a suprapubic incision, completing the extraction process.
The surgeon retrieves the spleen through a suprapubic incision, completing the removal process.
